metadata.title: The Prosecutor's Fallacy
metadata.notes: Calling Bullshit (Vox Style), film five. Expert's closing line and the five-innocent-matches arithmetic verified against the source ('the bigger the database, the more likely you'll find five people whose fingerprints match… even though only one of them is guilty'). Color law: crimson = false matches, ink = the one guilty, navy = the reading direction. Exclusions: no Bayes notation, no priors vocabulary, dragnets ONE beat, separate from the medical base-rate film. Outro pads A10.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: NO RENDERABLE BEATS: no beat carries a shot.remotion.pattern or Manim annotation — the pipeline will render NOTHING and every slot becomes a YOU-slate. The sheet was authored without the scene contract.
A01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
A10: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The expert takes the stand: the fingerprint matches, and the odds of a match this good by accident are one in ten million.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The expert takes the stand: the fingerprint matches, and the odds of a match this good by accident are one in ten million.

shot.note: cold open — courtroom from the jury box, witness stand lit, faces indistinct; shot.focus on the stand

beats.narration_text: One in ten million sounds like a conviction. Stay with the numbers, and it turns into reasonable doubt.
beats.tts_normalized_text: One in ten million sounds like a conviction. Stay with the numbers, and it turns into reasonable doubt.

card.title: THE PROSECUTOR'S FALLACY
card.sub: one in ten million — probably innocent

build.suggested: suggested prompt: One in ten million sounds like a conviction. Stay with the numbers, and it turns into reasonable doubt.

beats.narration_text: The print wasn't matched to a suspect. It was matched against a database — fifty million people.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The print wasn't matched to a suspect. It was matched against a database — fifty million people.

viz.note: isotype field standing for 50 million prints (scaled grid, count chip)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The print wasn't matched to a suspect. It was matched against a database — fifty million people.

beats.narration_text: Run it: at one in ten million, fifty million innocent prints produce five accidental matches.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Run it: at one in ten million, fifty million innocent prints produce five accidental matches.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Run it: at one in ten million, fifty million innocent prints produce five accidental matches.

beats.narration_text: So the police see six hits: five innocent, one guilty. The defendant is one of the six — five-to-one likely innocent.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So the police see six hits: five innocent, one guilty. The defendant is one of the six — five-to-one likely innocent.

viz.note: six squares surface from the field: five crimson (innocent), one ink (guilty)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So the police see six hits: five innocent, one guilty. The defendant is one of the six — five-to-one likely innocent.

beats.narration_text: The expert quoted the chance of a match given innocence. The jury needs the chance of innocence given a match. Those are different questions.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The expert quoted the chance of a match given innocence. The jury needs the chance of innocence given a match. Those are different questions.

viz.note: 2×2 table; navy highlight pivots from reading the row to reading the column

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The expert quoted the chance of a match given innocence. The jury needs the chance of innocence given a match. Those are different questions.

beats.narration_text: Lawyers know this mistake so well it has a name: the prosecutor's fallacy. Swapping the two questions can swap the verdict.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Lawyers know this mistake so well it has a name: the prosecutor's fallacy. Swapping the two questions can swap the verdict.

card.title: THE PROSECUTOR'S FALLACY
card.sub: swap the question, swap the verdict

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Lawyers know this mistake so well it has a name: the prosecutor's fallacy. Swapping the two questions can swap the verdict.

beats.narration_text: And the bigger the database, the worse it gets: more innocent prints, more accidental matches, more defendants who are simply unlucky.
beats.tts_normalized_text: And the bigger the database, the worse it gets: more innocent prints, more accidental matches, more defendants who are simply unlucky.

viz.note: the field grows; crimson matches multiply with it

build.suggested: suggested prompt: And the bigger the database, the worse it gets: more innocent prints, more accidental matches, more defendants who are simply unlucky.

beats.narration_text: The same arithmetic haunts DNA sweeps and face-recognition dragnets — huge databases, tiny error rates, crowds of innocent matches.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The same arithmetic haunts DNA sweeps and face-recognition dragnets — huge databases, tiny error rates, crowds of innocent matches.

shot.note: a wall of surveillance monitors, screens abstract, NO legible faces; shot.focus center screen

beats.narration_text: So when a match sounds impossible by chance, ask how many chances were taken. Rare times enormous equals routine.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So when a match sounds impossible by chance, ask how many chances were taken. Rare times enormous equals routine.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So when a match sounds impossible by chance, ask how many chances were taken. Rare times enormous equals routine.
